Name: information_theoretic_neural_network_compression
Title: Exploring Information-Theoretic Limits of Neural Network Compression
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that information-theoretic principles, specifically mutual information and rate-distortion theory, can be used to derive fundamental limits on the compressibility of neural networks, providing a framework for evaluating and improving compression algorithms.
Related Work: Recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing, Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation, and Geometric Signal Separation have explored training-free and geometric approaches to neural network compression and pruning. However, these methods lack a clear information-theoretic foundation, which our proposal aims to provide.
Abstract: This proposal explores the application of information-theoretic principles to neural network compression. We aim to derive fundamental limits on the compressibility of neural networks using mutual information and rate-distortion theory. Our approach will provide a framework for evaluating and improving compression algorithms, enabling the development of more efficient and effective compression methods. We will investigate the relationship between compression rate, distortion, and mutual information, and explore the use of information-theoretic bounds to guide the design of compression algorithms.
Experiments: Derive information-theoretic bounds on the compressibility of neural networks using mutual information and rate-distortion theory
Evaluate the performance of existing compression algorithms in relation to these bounds
Develop new compression algorithms guided by information-theoretic principles
Compare the performance of these algorithms with existing methods
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of deriving information-theoretic bounds for neural networks
The challenge of developing practical compression algorithms that approach these bounds
The potential for limited improvement in compression performance due to the fundamental limits imposed by information theory
